title: Coulomb blockade in microscopic material defects as a source of decoherence and noise in solid-state quantum circuits
abstract: A critical limitation of solid-state quantum devices arises from the materials from which they are fabricated: uncontrolled surfaces, interfaces, and structural imperfections introduce numerous sources of loss and decoherence. Despite extensive efforts, linking these decoherence mechanisms to their microscopic material origins -- essential for developing effective mitigation strategies -- remains an outstanding challenge that has slowed coherence improvements. Here, using scanning gate microscopy on live superconducting circuits we identify a previously unrecognised decoherence mechanism originating from Coulomb blockade and microwave-driven charge tunnelling in metallic grains. Such grains are ubiquitous in thin-film devices fabricated by standard lithography processes. By characterising multiple defects across different devices, we find such defects to be as common and as debilitating to device performance as two-level system (TLS) defects, while originating from a fundamentally different physical mechanism. Importantly, conventional characterisation techniques would misattribute this loss to other, microwave power-independent processes. Our observations thus reveal a widespread source of decoherence in superconducting circuits, challenging the prevailing paradigm that coherence lifetimes are primarily limited by TLS defects. Eliminating metallic grains during fabrication provides a clear and practical route to suppress this mechanism, offering a pathway towards improved coherence and reduced noise in microwave-based solid-state quantum devices.

bite.hook: A newly discovered defect in metallic grains is just as damaging to quantum circuits as two-level systems.
tldr: They investigated microscopic material defects causing decoherence in superconducting quantum circuits.
They used scanning gate microscopy on live superconducting circuits to locate and characterize individual defects.
They identified Coulomb blockade and charge tunneling in metallic grains as a major, previously unrecognized source of noise.
bite.whyItMatters: Superconducting quantum computers are highly sensitive to noise. Identifying this hidden metallic grain defect gives engineers a clear, practical fabrication fix to boost quantum coherence lifetimes.
bite.eli5: Imagine trying to build a house of cards, but tiny, invisible vibrations keep knocking it down. Scientists used a super-precise microscope to find that tiny, accidental metal crumbs left over from manufacturing are acting like microscopic electrical traps, shaking up the delicate quantum states.

glossary.shortDef: The increased resistance to electronic transport at small bias voltage due to electrostatic repulsion.
glossary.eli5Def: A traffic jam for electrons where one electron blocks others from entering a tiny space because of their matching negative charges.

glossary.shortDef: The loss of quantum coherence due to interaction with the surrounding environment.
glossary.eli5Def: When a quantum computer's delicate information gets scrambled because of outside noise, like a spinning top getting bumped.

glossary.shortDef: A microscopic defect in quantum materials that absorbs energy and causes decoherence.
glossary.eli5Def: Tiny atomic defects that act like microscopic tuning forks, stealing energy from the quantum computer and ruining its calculations.
